course_title: Peace and Conflict Amidst Global Disorder
course_description:
Peace and Conflict Studies is an interdisciplinary field of study with a commitment to the non-violent management and resolution of conflict from the local to the international level. To pursue this goal, core political concepts such as power, order, violence and justice are theorized in distinctive ways. This course develops an understanding of key theoretical approaches in peace and conflict studies research, and enables students to understand the relevance of theory to practice in this field. Students will engage in critical dialogue on questions such as how we identify actors in peace and in conflict, and how we can work against differing forms of violence and reconstitute just and participatory social and political order in the wake of violence.

POLS2515 examines how the field of Peace and Conflict Studies has evolved and how it is responding to a rapidly changing global context. As the contemporary world order shifts, these changes are reshaping power, inequality, justice, violence, and peace at national, local, and regional levels, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region.

The course explores how macro-level global changes interact with micro-level conflict dynamics and situates contemporary peace and conflict theories and practices within these shifting contexts. This enables students to develop the ability to critically assess recent changes, continuities, and debates in the field of Peace and Conflict Studies.

Through interactive lectures, tutorials, practitioner engagement, and assessments, students engage with some of the field’s central questions. These include: Why are some conflicts more intractable than others? How do authoritarian and illiberal states manage conflict, and what kinds of peace do they produce? What roles do young people play in shaping peace and conflict locally and regionally? How is artificial intelligence reshaping peace and conflict dynamics? How can local infrastructures for peace be built and sustained, and by whom? Finally, the course examines growing calls to decolonise peace and conflict studies and what this means for theory, research, and practice.

Lectures and seminar:
The course includes 12 two-hour lectures and one two-hours seminar. Lectures start in Week 1.ﾠ

There will be 8 tutorials starting
after the Week 3 lecture.
See the 'Learning Activities' section for more details.ﾠﾠ
course_aims: POLS2515 aims to provide students with knowledge and skills to engage with and navigate complex theories, concepts and practices in peace and conflict studies. This course provides students with the conceptual tools necessary to engage in the study of peace and conflict and with which to analyse and make sense of current issues, debates and associated developments in the field.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand how changing global order has shaped peace and conflict issues and debates in the contemporary world.
Interpret how contemporary conflicts affect people across cultures and societies.
Critically engage with diverse communities of scholars and practitioners for advancing peace and positive social change.
Explain how ethical theories are justified and inform peace practices and vice versa.

assessment_details.title: Course participation and engagement
assessment_details.task_description: Task description: This assessment has two parts . Part 1: Tutorial participation (16 marks) The first part of this assessment is tutorial participation and is worth 16 marks. In this part, you should participate actively in tutorial discussions. You will receive 2 marks for participating in each tutorial, in which you are expected to: Read the required reading materials before tutorials and be prepared to engage with discussion questions. Express your own ideas about the content. Show an interest in others’ thoughts and contribute to their development or offer counterarguments. Ask questions for clarification or elaboration. Demonstrate knowledge of the content covered in lectures and tutorials. If you are unable to attend a tutorial due to extraordinary circumstances, you may have the opportunity to make up the missed mark by submitting a short review of two required readings for that week. You must discuss this with the course coordinator first and then submit the review to your tutor by Friday of the following week. Ongoing work or study commitments are not considered valid reasons. Part 2: Learning reflection (4 marks) The second part of this assessment consists of a written learning reflection, due on Friday 24 April (Week 8). Word length: Maximum 350 words. In the reflective piece, your focus should be on what you learned about ethical use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Peace and Conflict Studies research and writing. This should be based on course readings and tutorials and discussions from the seminar in Week 7. Citations and references are not required, but you may include a small number if they help support your reflections. You may write in the first-person language. The reflections will not be formally assessed, and no feedback will be provided, but they will be checked to ensure you have completed the exercise and are eligible to receive the mark. A very short reflection (for example, less than 100 words) will receive a mark of 0. This part of the assessment must be submitted via Turnitin. Refer to the Blackboard site for further information. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin .
I n uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission . Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Contact Course Coordinator directly to discuss your situation if you are unable attend a tutorial or submit the written learning reflections.

assessment_details.title: Conflict Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description: Task description: In this assessment, students are required to choose and analyse a conflict case of their choice. The case must fall into one of the following categories: a protracted conflict (drawing on the Week 3 topic), or a conflict driven primarily by economic factors (drawing on the Week 4 topic), or a recently ended conflict (drawing on the Week 5 topic). Your case analysis must focus primarily on conflict dynamics and processes (avoid elaborate descriptions or extensive historical and economic context), using relevant concepts and theories discussed in the course. It should be clearly structured and organised. You may consider including the following parts in your structure. Introduction: Briefly introduce your chosen case by identifying the conflict and its context, outlining the key dynamics of the conflict (such as main actors, causes, patterns, or consequences), and clearly stating the focus of your analysis. Main body: Apply one key theory or a set of closely related theoretical concepts (selected from the unit readings and lectures) relevant to your case. This part should include your main case analysis, applying a concept or theory to illustrate the nature and dynamics of the conflict. Conclusion: Reflect briefly on your use of theory by explaining how the selected theory or concepts helped you understand the case, and discuss the extent to which the theory was useful or limited in analysing the conflict. The total word length is 1500 words, excluding references (a 10% leeway is accepted). Refer to the Blackboard site for marking criteria and the Turnitin submission link. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.

Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension is granted, penalties for late submission apply. Students are penalised 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item for every calendar day that an assessment item is late.
Marks will be deducted each day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point the submitted item will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is triggered from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.title: Policy Briefing Paper
assessment_details.task_description: In no more than 1500 words, write a policy briefing paper that responds to the following question: Why does Australia need a clear conflict prevention strategy, and what approaches should such a strategy include to help prevent conflicts in the Asia–Pacific region? This assessment requires you to apply theories and concepts from the course to a real-world policy challenge. Your task is to analyse Australia’s current approach to conflict prevention and make a case for Australia’s conflict prevention strategy. You should imagine that you are writing this piece for a non-academic audience, specifically, the community of policymakers in Australia. This means your writing should be clear, concise, and accessible. In your briefing paper, consider including the following: Explain why a conflict prevention strategy matters for Australia in today’s regional and global context. Examine Australia’s current approaches and strategies for conflict prevention, identifying strengths and gaps. Draw lessons from other countries’ strategies (e.g., the UK, Sweden, Norway) and consider whether Australia could learn from these approaches. Propose key approaches or elements that a stronger conflict prevention strategy should include for Australia in the Asia–Pacific region. Conclude with a small number of practical policy recommendations. Your proposed approach does not need to be detailed or comprehensive; instead, outline principles, priorities, and possible areas for action. Support your analysis with relevant concepts and debates from the course. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: End of Semester Exam
assessment_details.task_description: The end of semester exam is worth 30 % of the total mark. Timing: Examination period (6-20 June) The exam will take place in person and on campus during the exam period. UQ will schedule the date and time of the written exam during the examination period (6-20 June). You will be required to write three short answers (350 - 400 words maximum) from a choice of seven questions (each answer is worth 10 marks, i.e. 30 marks in total). The exam questions will be based on topics covered in the course. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: How is the field of Peace and Conflict studies shifting amidst global (dis)order?
Is liberal peace era over?
What is conflict and why are some conflicts more intractable than others?
Tutorial 1: Intractable conflicts
How does economy shape peace and conflict dynamics?
Tutorial 2: Economy, conflict and peace
How do armed conflicts end?
Tutorial 3 - Tips and techniques for writing case study analysis.
How do authoritarian states manage conflicts within their territories?
MID-SEMESTER BREAK
What ethical issues arise when using AI in peace and conflict research?
Tutorial 4: Peace and conflict in authoritarian and illiberal states
Does conflict prevention promote the sustaining peace agenda?
Tutorial 5: Australia's role in conflict prevention in the Asia Pacific
How does climate change, impact upon conflict and peace?
Tutorial 6: Climate change, peace and conflict
Does the concept of ‘infrastructures for peace’ (I4P) have a future?
How is artificial intelligence shaping the future of peace and conflict?
Tutorial 7: Peace and conflcit in the age of AI.
Why is the voice for decolonising Peace and Conflict Studies growing?
Tutorial 8: Decolonising Peace and Conflict Studies
Course conclusion: What does peace mean amidst global disorder?
